title: St. Sarah the Hermit, St. Helias of Egypt, St. Aristobulus of Britain & St. Agapius and His Seven Companions
primary_saint: St. Sarah the Hermit of the Nile, St. Helias of Egypt, St. Aristobulus of Britain, and St. Agapius with His Seven Companions
narrative:
On the fifteenth of Megabit, the midpoint of the month, the holy Church places before the faithful four witnesses who together span the full geographic range of the Christian world, from the banks of the Nile to the far edge of Britain, bound together by the common thread of the ascetic vocation lived in its most geographically extreme expressions. Saint Sarah the Hermit is one of the most celebrated of the desert mothers, an ammas of the Nile whose spiritual authority was recognised by the male desert fathers who sought her counsel, and who is preserved in the Apophthegmata Patrum for two sayings that have resonated across the centuries. When male monks sought to test her by suggesting that she must not allow herself too much pride given that she was only a woman, she replied: According to nature I am a woman, but not according to my thoughts. And when asked how she had survived so long in the desert, she replied simply: I put my foot out to mount the ladder of life, and I put death before my eyes before going up it. These sayings define the desert tradition's understanding of the relationship between gender, grace, and the interior life.

Saint Helias of Egypt is the second desert father of this day's assembly: a hermit of Lower Egypt who lived in the delta region and was known for a particular gift of discernment — the ability to see the state of a soul with a clarity that his visitors found alternately comforting and disturbing. The tradition records that Helias spent sixty years in the desert before his death, and that in those sixty years he received an education in the human condition that no academic institution could have provided — every variety of temptation, delusion, genuine holiness, and spiritual counterfeit passing through his cell in the persons of those who came to consult him. His Lenten significance lies in his longevity: sixty years of Lent, in a sense, and still he called himself a beginner.

Saint Aristobulus of Britain is this day's great geographical discovery: one of the seventy apostles, a brother of the Apostle Barnabas and a companion of the Apostle Paul, who according to the tradition preserved in the Synaxarium was sent as the first bishop and apostle to Britain — to the far northwestern edge of the Roman world, to a cold island at the end of the known earth. The tradition is not unanimous about the details, but the figure of an apostle of the first generation reaching Britain, planting the faith in a land of forests and fog, founding a community that would survive for centuries in isolation from the Mediterranean world: this figure has been treasured by the British church and by the Ethiopian church alike as a sign of the absolute universality of the apostolic commission, which recognised no frontier as terminal and no people as unreachable.

Saint Agapius and his seven companions are a company of martyrs whose collective witness is honoured in the Megabit Synaxarium as one of those group testimonies in which the community of faith is more visible than any individual. Agapius was a bishop or senior presbyter whose community was arrested together and executed together, refusing to separate themselves from each other even in the face of death. The tradition notes that they encouraged one another throughout their imprisonment and their final ordeal, and that each one's endurance made the others stronger — a testimony to the corporate nature of the Christian witness that the solitary martyriological tradition can obscure. Sarah who climbed the ladder, Helias who discerned for sixty years, Aristobulus who reached Britain, Agapius who held his community together to the end: four expressions of the single apostolic fidelity. Amen.
